Vegetable prices surge in Hyderabad amid heatwave, unseasonal rains
Vegetable prices in Hyderabad have surged sharply due to extreme heat and unseasonal rains affecting crop yield and supply. Tomato prices have nearly tripled, while other vegetables also witnessed hikes. Prices may remain high until the monsoon improves supply conditions.
Hyderabad: With a scorching summer and unseasonal rains, the vegetable supplies to the city have been hit, and prices are skyrocketing. In the last week, the prices of vegetables have shot up in the city markets, with rates of tomatoes almost increasing three times.
A kilogram of tomato was sold for Rs 10 in the retail market a fortnight ago, it increased to Rs 20 a week ago and suddenly it is now sold for Rs 30 or Rs 40 in the retail market.
“The crop is badly hit due to the heat conditions and the unseasonal heavy rains that lashed last week. The tomato yield has come down, and the supply to the city is heavily affected. Hence, the price increased,” said Ramu, a farmer from Yacharam, in Ranga Reddy district.
The prices of other vegetables such as lady finger, brinjal, cauliflower, beans, cabbage and leafy vegetables have also seen a significant increase in the last week. “The supplies have come down from the villages, hence the shortage and price rise. It will continue until the monsoon sets in,” pointed out S Muthyala, a commission agent at Mandi Mir Alam.
A kilogram of lady finger is now priced at Rs 70, up from Rs 50 a fortnight ago. Cabbage is sold for Rs 40 a kilogram, brinjal – Rs 50 a kilogram, beans priced between Rs 80 and Rs 120 a kilogram, and leafy vegetables are priced at Rs 10 per medium-sized bundle.
“There is a short supply of coriander, mint herb, rosella leaves, and other leafy vegetables. Due to this, it is sold for Rs 10 each in the retail market,” said Anjamma, a vegetable vendor at Falaknuma Road.
The city gets vegetable supplies from Balapur, Ibrahimpatnam, Kandukur, Yacharam, and Amangal mandals in Ranga Reddy district and some mandals in neighbouring Vikarabad district, Yadadri Bhuvanagiri and Siddipet district.
Every morning, the farmers bring the vegetables in small transport jeeps and sell them to the local vendors at the Bowenpally, Gudimalkapur, Madannapet, and Mir Alam Mandi markets. Some farmers directly sell the produce to customers at the Rythu Bazaars in the city.

Gaming in extreme heat: Summer survival guide for Indian gamers
With extreme heat and rising power challenges, gaming in India this summer requires caution. This guide outlines practical tips to prevent device overheating, manage power risks, and ensure safe gaming across mobile, PC, and console platforms.
Hyderabad: “Hot, hotter, and hottest” is my go-to three-word phrase to describe the current weather in Telangana. As we brace for extreme conditions across the country, we must also anticipate the challenges of the predicted El Niño effect during monsoon time. In what is likely to become a gruelling, arid year, gaming for Indian players will be increasingly difficult as we face significant hurdles in cooling our devices and combating the ever-present dust.
Adding to these meteorological complexities are the unique challenges of fuel acquisition due to the ongoing war in the Gulf, which has rendered both direct power generation and backup systems more expensive and unpredictable. Before this devolves into a column about why 2026 is one of our hardest years yet, I would like to refocus on the central purpose of this article: the best practices a gamer can adhere to in the relentless heat of this year’s summer.
Essentials to consider:
Playing video games in cool, dark environments is the best possible strategy. For those without access to air conditioning, early mornings (before 8 am) and late evenings (after 9 pm) are the most viable times to play. Ensure you are gaming in open spaces with sufficient airflow to dissipate heat from gaming systems. For those with air conditioning, I recommend exercising caution; given the heightened risk of power cuts, it is vital to have suitable power and cooling backups in place.
If you rely on UPS or inverter systems, now is the time to check if your batteries and power capacities are in a healthy condition. A sudden loss of power while a system is under heavy load can be detrimental to internal components. Finally, please make it a habit to periodically check your device’s temperature – even by hand if necessary. If the chassis or CPU tower feels excessively hot to the touch, it is prudent to shut down the system immediately to prevent long-term hardware damage.
Device-specific concerns:
For mobile and handheld gamers, playing while charging your device is a strict “no.” Regardless of your charging method – whether via wall charger, power bank, or wirelessly – batteries naturally generate heat during a charge cycle. Combining this with the thermal load of a demanding game is a hazardous combination. This also applies to laptop gamers; it is sensible to review your power profiles and thermal settings for the summer months.
For PC gamers with high-end configurations, liquid cooling is mandatory in this climate. Console gamers, meanwhile, must ensure maximum airflow. I strongly advise against playing graphically intense, demanding titles without air conditioning between 9 am and 9 pm. Because consoles and compact game systems have limited internal space for heat dissipation, you must ensure that their vents are completely unobstructed and free of dust.
Final Pointers:
Irrespective of your primary gaming device, a few final pointers can significantly improve your experience this summer. Consider playing at slightly lower resolutions, frame rates, and visual settings than usual; marginally lowering screen brightness and avoiding multitasking can also help reduce the thermal load.
For consoles and PCs, avoid using “Rest” or “Sleep” modes, as these still generate passive heat. Furthermore, try to avoid marathon gaming sessions, opting instead for shorter, limited windows throughout the day.
With the soaring prices of hardware in 2026, it is paramount that we protect our devices to the best of our abilities. I hope you all have a fantastic summer of gaming, one where you can enjoy as many worlds as possible – responsibly and cautiously.

articles.title: Telangana rolls out heatwave action plan; schools to implement water bells, cooling centres set up - Dailyhunt
articles.full_text:
Hyderabad: As Telangana prepare for heatwave, all schools have been instructed to implement 'water bells' and malls, religious places and public buildings have been designated as 'cooling centres' for the general public seeking reprieve from the hot sun.
It is now mandatory for schools to provide 'water bells', with breaks to ensure the students are well hydrated. Schools have also been asked not to hold open-air classes, which will ensure children do not come in direct contact with sun. The Aanganwadi centers across Hyderabad and State will now operate only until 11 am
Malls and shops have been instructed by the State government to keep cold water at their premises, while religious places, government buildings, and panchayat offices have been designated as cooling centres, where the public can go and rest during the heat wave. It has also been decided to install green shades at traffic junctions and public gatherings.
Health Minister, Damodar Raja Narasimha issued a comprehensive set of instructions to District Collectors to mitigate the risks of heatwave. Beyond students and the general public, special focus is on 'gig economy workers, with specific outreach planned for auto-drivers, cab drivers, and delivery workers who are at high risk during peak afternoon hours.
All the government hospitals should have a dedicated heatstroke team in the emergency area to begin treatment immediately upon a patient’s arrival. The Minister has also directed the electricity department to frame a summer-specific power load plan to ensure uninterrupted electricity to hospitals and water supply units, while the Fire Department conducts audits of chemical godowns to prevent heat-induced fires.
The Telangana State Development Planning Society (TGDPS) will now provide Mandal-level, two-day forecasts focusing on the Heat Index, which is a combination of temperature and humidity, and to be shared directly to local nodal officers.
Authorities have warned of an 'Orange Alert' condition already prevailing in several districts, with May and early June expected to see sustained maximum temperatures between 41 degree Celsius and 44 degree Celsius.
Residents are urged to avoid outdoor activities between 12 noon and 3 pm and utilize the newly established 'Chalivendrams' (drinking water kiosks) being set up across urban and rural congregation points, authorities urged.
- Malls, public buildings and religious places to serve water and cooling centers
- Mandatory water bells in schools and avoid open-air classes
- Aanganwadi centres to be shut down by 11 am
- Shift schedule of outdoor workers from 12 noon to 3 pm during heart wave alert
- Fire department to conduct security check at commercial complexes and hospitals
- Shaded areas and cool water tubs for animals in urban and rural areas
- Veterinary services to treat heat stress animals

articles.title: Hyderabad’s 46°C Heatwave Meets Sudden Storms: Should You Cancel Your Weekend Plans? - NativePlanet
articles.full_text:
Hyderabad and Telangana face an intense heatwave with temperatures nearing 46 degrees today. The Indian Meteorological Department issued alerts for possible evening thunderstorms across the entire state. These fast shifts are forcing many residents to rethink their weekend travel plans right now. Staying indoors or finding cooler escapes is currently the main priority for many families.
Severe heat makes outdoor activities during the day nearly impossible for city dwellers. Local tourists are now searching for short-haul hill stays to beat the rising mercury. The sudden storm alert adds a layer of complexity to these last-minute journey decisions. It creates a mix of extreme dry heat followed by potential heavy rain later tonight.
Impact of Hyderabad Heat and Telangana Thunderstorm Alerts
The mercury is rising rapidly across the state, creating very taxing conditions for residents. Officials advise people to stay hydrated and avoid direct sun during peak afternoon hours. This record-breaking heat is part of a larger trend affecting the southern regions this summer. Many families are choosing to travel after sunset to avoid the most intense sun exposure.
Most districts are bracing for a dry day followed by wind and rain. The IMD predicts localized thunderstorms and gusty winds across certain parts of Telangana today. These storms can provide temporary relief but also disrupt local transport and flight schedules. Careful monitoring of weather apps is essential for those planning any outdoor weekend events.
The high humidity before the storm can make the heat feel even more oppressive. This phenomenon often leads to a sudden drop in pressure and rising cloud formations. Such erratic weather patterns are typical during the pre-monsoon season on the plateau. Understanding these cycles helps travelers choose the best time to hit the road safely.
Escape to Hill Stations as Hyderabad Temperatures Soar
Hill stations like Ananthagiri Hills are becoming popular choices for those seeking immediate relief. These spots offer a noticeable drop in temperature and lush green surroundings for visitors. Recently, many travelers are booking resorts that provide indoor cooling and scenic forest views. Short-distance trips remain the most viable option for a quick two-day weekend escape.
Planning a trip requires looking at the current temperature trends in nearby destinations. While Hyderabad stays hot, elevated regions provide a much cooler and fresher environment for tourists. This contrast is driving a surge in last-minute hotel bookings away from the city. Travelers are prioritizing locations that offer a significant break from the blistering urban heat.
Here is a quick comparison of the current temperatures in and around the city. These figures show why residents are looking for alternatives to urban life this weekend. Lower temperatures in the hills provide the perfect excuse for a sudden road trip. These variations influence how people decide to pack and prepare for their journey.
Driving in extreme heat requires checking vehicle coolant levels and tire pressure before starting. Thunderstorms can also make hilly roads slippery and visibility quite poor for many drivers. Carrying extra water and essential medicines is vital for those heading towards remote areas. Safety should always come first when navigating through such unpredictable and harsh weather patterns.
Travelers should also consider the impact of gusty winds on camping or trekking activities. Many local parks and forest reserves might have restricted access during heavy storm warnings. It is best to check with local authorities before visiting any nature-based tourist spots. Staying informed ensures that your weekend getaway remains enjoyable despite the changing weather.
The combination of high heat and sudden storms defines this unique weekend in Telangana. While the weather remains challenging, a well-planned trip can still offer much-needed relaxation. Keep an eye on local weather updates to ensure a safe and comfortable journey. This season demands flexibility and caution for every traveler seeking a respite from the sun.

articles.title: Mendora records 46°C as Telangana reels under heat - Hyderabad Mail
articles.full_text:
HYDERABAD : The state continues to reel under intense summer heat, with conditions resembling a heatwave across several regions. Residents are facing high temperatures and severe humidity.
Maximum temperatures above 41°C have been recorded in most parts of the state. Mendora in Nizamabad district recorded the highest temperature of 46°C on Sunday.
The weather department said similar conditions are likely to continue for the next two days and advised people to take precautions. It added that heatwave conditions may prevail in north Telangana districts.
Teen dies of heatstroke in Nalgonda district
In a separate incident, a teenager died of suspected heatstroke in Nalgonda district. The deceased was identified as Matangi Vikram, 16, son of Matangi Madaru and Mutyalamma of Sangaram village.
Temperatures across key locations (°C)
- Bhadrachalam – 37.5
